data.source: Quantitative online survey administered through an ISO-certified online access panel
data.sample_size: 1,242 respondents after data cleansing (1,300 initial completions)
data.unit_of_analysis: Individual adult residents of Germany (18–74 years old, self-reported not infected with COVID-19)
data.access_details: Data collected by a commercial panel provider that handled programming and recruitment; no public access details provided
data.notes: Responses completed in full; any case finishing in under seven minutes was removed during data cleansing

method.description: A cross-sectional online questionnaire measured Germans’ COVID-19 risk perceptions, coping strategies, emergency food preparedness, and socio-demographics. All items were compulsory. Descriptive statistics, scale construction, and bivariate tests (e.g., gender and age comparisons, correlations) were used to analyse the data.
steps: Design questionnaire covering: (a) risk perception (cognitive, affective, psychometric dimensions), (b) coping (problem-focused and emotion-focused items based on the German Ways of Coping Scale plus specific behaviours), (c) emergency food preparedness, and (d) demographics.
Program survey in the panel provider’s platform; make all questions mandatory.
Recruit a quota-sample representative of Germany by gender, age, and federal state via the online panel.
Clean data by deleting respondents who completed in under seven minutes, yielding N=1,242.
Compute descriptive statistics (means, standard deviations, percentages).
Create problem-focused and emotion-focused coping scales; assess internal consistency (Cronbach’s α).
Conduct subgroup comparisons (e.g., t-tests for gender; correlations with age) and report significant differences.
method.models: Descriptive statistics; independent-samples t-tests; Pearson correlations
method.outcome_variable: Primary outcomes include perceived risk of infection (three Likert items), fear/worry about COVID-19, coping scale scores, and emergency food storage behaviour
method.independent_variables: Age, gender, place of residence (federal state, urbanicity)

results.summary: Respondents perceived higher infection risk for family/friends than for themselves. Older adults rated their own infection risk lower than younger adults. Women were more worried about COVID-19 and showed higher agreement with both emotion-focused and problem-focused coping strategies than men. Problem-focused coping (e.g., following experts’ advice) was generally more prevalent than emotion-focused coping. Most participants reported increased hand hygiene and avoidance of public places. Half of the sample stored food for 10–11 days, and 41.9 % reported buying more food than usual, mainly to minimise shopping trips or prepare for possible quarantine.

numerical_results.outcome_name: Perceived likelihood of self-infection in near future (1=very likely, 5=not very likely)
Perceived likelihood of family/friends infection in near future

metadata.original_paper_title: COVID-19: Risk perception and Coping strategies. Results from a survey in Germany.
